rationale: The cited paper is not only listed in background citations; it is explicitly used in the methods section where the authors state they are adapting a manipulation from prior offer-customization research (Sela, Simonson, and Kivetz 2013). This indicates a concrete design role in how the citing paper operationalizes unique versus popular options. The citing paper does not engage the unintended-value mechanism from the cited work, so the link is methodological/conceptual borrowing rather than a direct theoretical extension.
relation_description: The citing paper applies the cited paper's offer-customization paradigm to build its own experimental choice manipulation (customized vs popular recommendations) in testing smartphone effects on uniqueness preference. It uses the cited work as support for customization as a uniqueness-expression option, without extending its core unintended-value theory.
